title: Will You Pause and Think, at Least Now?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, punctuation, party-name, and word-break errors have been repaired. Historical allegations and press reports remain attributed. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: At every campaign meeting, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa repeatedly asks what the DMK, which participated in Central governments for nearly seventeen years, did for Tamil Nadu's people. Our election manifesto answered by listing the projects the DMK sought and secured from the Centre. When she continued asking, we publicly repeated that list. Yet she still asks the same question at every meeting.

Participation in the Central government is not even essential to securing certain rights and achievements. At one time, the Governor alone hoisted the flag at the Secretariat on Independence Day. It was at my request that Chief Ministers in every state obtained the right to hoist it instead. The DMK was not then part of the Central government.

Even when we participated at the Centre, we understood the DMK's strength, placed Tamil Nadu's requests accordingly and secured them. That is why the DMK retains an all-India reputation as a good ally. What reputation does the AIADMK have?

In the 1998 Lok Sabha election, the AIADMK, BJP, PMK and MDMK were allied in Tamil Nadu. Even before polling, AIADMK Presidium Chairman Navalar openly declared: “If the BJP comes to power, we shall demand the dismissal of the DMK government in Tamil Nadu.”

Explaining the alliance, BJP leader Vajpayee told reporters that if corruption charges were proved in the appropriate courts, the BJP would sever relations with accused leaders, including former Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa.

BJP General Secretary Govindacharya called the electoral understanding with Jayalalithaa's AIADMK a “deviation.” He said the BJP had made two deviations, appointing unsuitable people to the Uttar Pradesh Cabinet and allying with the AIADMK, and had done so for self-protection.

Before the election, on 14 February 1998, bombs exploded at thirteen places in Coimbatore, killing more than thirty people. Two exploded near the stage where Advani was to speak, killing ten. The incident greatly harmed the DMK alliance. It won ten seats and the AIADMK alliance thirty. Nationally, the BJP alliance won 254 seats, Congress 168, the United Front ninety-three and other parties seventeen. Vajpayee was chosen BJP parliamentary leader.

Jayalalithaa met him in Delhi on 9 March and then told reporters the DMK government in Tamil Nadu must be dismissed. Asked about Sonia Gandhi possibly becoming Congress leader, she replied that it would be a tragedy for the country and asked whether, among India's 980 million people, no one else possessed the ability and qualification to lead.

President K.R. Narayanan asked Vajpayee to appear on 11 March with the list and documents of supporting MPs. Vajpayee could not meet him at the appointed time. Why? Other parties had submitted letters, but support letters from the AIADMK, PMK and others in Tamil Nadu had not arrived.

Though she had promised unconditional support, newspapers of 13 March reported that Jayalalithaa demanded a guarantee that the DMK government would be dismissed, withdrawal of all cases against her, the Finance portfolio for Subramanian Swamy and Law for Vazhapadi Ramamurthy before giving her letter. They said the BJP leadership firmly rejected those conditions. Jayalalithaa denied the reports and said the delay arose because she awaited a favourable response to Tamil Nadu's needs.

On 14 March she said the AIADMK and allies had sent support letters but would not join the Ministry, complaining that the BJP had disrespected and humiliated her. On 15 March she reversed herself and said the AIADMK, PMK and Vazhapadi Congress would participate. Thambidurai, Sedapatti Muthiah, Vazhapadi Ramamurthy, Rangarajan Kumaramangalam, Dalit Ezhilmalai, Janarthanan and R.K. Kumar became Ministers.

Our manifesto lists what the DMK brought Tamil Nadu while at the Centre. What, by contrast, was the character of the AIADMK's participation in 1998? What projects did it seek and obtain for Tamil Nadu?

Thambidurai obtained appointment orders for thirty-three AIADMK lawyers selected by Jayalalithaa to conduct Central government cases in the Madras High Court. They were summoned to her residence and she personally handed each order to them. The Statesman mocked this.

Within a month, she removed AIADMK Union Minister Sedapatti Muthiah and announced Dindigul Srinivasan in his place. Having removed Sedapatti, she demanded that the Prime Minister immediately dismiss other Union Ministers facing allegations. With no alternative, the Prime Minister removed Buta Singh.

After meeting the Prime Minister in Delhi on 2 April, she returned to Chennai and declared at the airport that they would insist upon dismissal of the DMK government. During the Prime Minister's reply to debate on the President's Address, Pranab Mukherjee, then a Congress member and now President, asked about Union Minister Rangarajan Kumaramangalam's statement that Tamil Nadu would soon be “liberated” from DMK rule. Vajpayee replied that his government firmly opposed imposing President's Rule in any state.

Planning Commission Deputy Chairman Jaswant Singh met Jayalalithaa in Chennai on 25 April and said she had agreed to attend the coming coordination committee. Dinamani's front page next day carried an article headed “Kaikeyi's Victory, the People's Defeat.”

Another “great achievement” of the AIADMK at the Centre came through an order by one of its Union Ministers on 7 May: eighty-nine Deputy Commissioners and 108 Assistant Commissioners were transferred, including officers investigating Jayalalithaa's income-tax cases in Chennai. Perhaps because he still failed to rescue her adequately from the cases, that Minister left office within days.

P. Chidambaram said in Chennai on 13 June that the AIADMK demanded the DMK government's dismissal every day and the question was whether the BJP government would succumb. Home Minister Advani said “no” would be the answer. Jayalalithaa responded that it was painful to have a Home Minister unconcerned with national security.
On 3 July, Dinamani's editorial “A Humiliation for Tamil Nadu” said destabilising the Central government had become the AIADMK's achievement.

The Statesman reported on 15 July that, following a temporary truce, the Vajpayee government had begun taking steps that could free Jayalalithaa from income-tax and other cases. Commissioner S.C. Jadhav, who supervised cases against her, was transferred to a prestigious Mumbai post and replaced by K. Gopalan. P.K. Sridharan, described as an honest officer, was transferred and replaced by N.P. Tripathi. Income-tax officials alleged these changes could provide temporary relief to Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and relatives. The report said the BJP obtained needed support by placating Jayalalithaa, while the demand to dismiss the DMK government was temporarily suspended. The Economic Times published a similar article on 21 July.
On 26 February 1999, AIADMK MPs met Prime Minister Vajpayee and pressed for the Petroleum portfolio then held by Vazhapadi Ramamurthy to be allotted to their party.

Judicial changes in late 1998 also drew press attention. The Tamil Maanila Congress led by our dear friend Moopanar resolved that, although courts including the Supreme Court had found sufficient evidence to prosecute corruption cases against Jayalalithaa, former Ministers and officials, the Prime Minister's Office called them political vengeance, showing the BJP government would do anything to preserve office.

Despite all this, Jayalalithaa met the President and withdrew support from the BJP government. When reporters asked me on 15 April 1999 about her pressure upon it, I issued a long statement compiling recommendations she had written in her own hand about which officials should be transferred and who should receive which post.

Jayalalithaa, who asks at every meeting what the DMK did at the Centre, we did not make demands like yours. We secured the Sethusamudram Project for Tamil Nadu; won Classical Language status for Tamil; obtained the Chennai Port-Maduravoyal elevated road, Chennai Metro Rail, the Hogenakkal Combined Water Supply Scheme and the National Maritime University. We brought countless projects like these.

Do you understand the truth at least now? What did we do, and what havoc did you create? To what extent did you torment a Central government? On the day the AIADMK left the alliance, Prime Minister Vajpayee reportedly said, with a wounded heart, “Tonight I shall sleep peacefully,” and described the AIADMK alliance as the most painful period of his political life.
What qualification does Chief Minister Jayalalithaa then have to speak of coalitions, friendship or participation in Central government? I ask Tamil Nadu's people to pause and think, at least now.
With affection,
M.K.
23 March 2014
